raw_case:
Prenatal diagnosis of the rarest conjoint twin “diprosopus tetrophthalmus” during anomaly scan: A case report
Twin pregnancy is considered high-risk pregnancy because of its various effects on maternal and fetal physiology. Twin pregnancy can be dizygotic or monozygotic, the latter being less common. Depending upon the period of separation of an embryo, monozygotic twins can share amniotic cavity and placenta with the monochorionic monoamniotic form being the least common type. Diprosopus tetrophthalmus is the rarest form of monozygotic monochorionic and monoamniotic conjoint twin, where there are 2 faces on 1 head with various degrees of duplication of facial and cranial structure. The exact etiology of diprosopus is still unknown however; there are many local environmental and oxidative theories for this anomaly. The incidence of diprosopus is 1 in 180,000 to 15 million births. Here we present a case of 20-year-old female who came for routine anomaly scan of her pregnancy in our hospital which showed the presence of diprosopus tetrophthalmus. Couples agreed to terminate pregnancy after proper counseling from treating physician. Ultrasonography images of anomaly scans as well as post-abortion images of the fetus are discussed in this case report.

Introduction

Twin pregnancy can be dizygotic where 2 ova are fertilized by 2 sperm or monozygotic where 1 ovum is fertilized by 1 sperm. Depending upon period of separation of an embryo during pregnancy, the monozygotic twin can share placenta and amniotic cavity with resultant dichorionic diamniotic, monochorionic diamniotic and monochorionic monoamniotic types of twin pregnancy. Diprosopus is the rarest type of conjoint twin belonging to the monochorionic monoamniotic group with a reported incidence of 1 in 180,000 to 15 million births [1]. There is a characteristic presence of 2 faces on a single head and trunk with variable degrees of duplication of facial and cranial structure [1]. It is commonly associated with anencephaly, spinal bifida, and various other cardiac and skeletal malformations. Anomaly scan in the expert hand plays a vital role in prenatal diagnosis of this entity. Here we present a case of diprosopus tetrophthalmus with spinal bifida and bilateral club foot diagnosed at 17 weeks of period of gestation.

Case presentation

A 20 year female G1P0 at 18 weeks of period of gestation came for her routine follow-up examination of her pregnancy. Her medical history was unremarkable with no history of intake of any drug other than iron and calcium for her pregnancy. She took vitamin folic acid during the preconceptional period and in the first trimester. She was non-alcoholic and non-smoker. On clinical examination, her vital was stable with unremarkable clinical examination. Her routine lab parameters including complete blood count and serology were within normal limits. Her dating scan was done outside in local hospital at 7 week of gestation, with normal cardiac activity and no other relevant findings. Anomaly scan was advised as part of normal routine evaluation of her pregnancy. Anomaly scan revealed duplicated lateral ventricle with dilated ventricular system and thinned-out brain parenchyma (Fig. 1A) suggestive of duplicated frontal lobe. The skull vault was deficient in the antero-superior aspect suggestive of acrania (Figs. 1A and 4). Two thalami were present which were not fused (Fig. 1A). Four eyes were noted in the face with 2 placed in the midline and 2 in the lateral aspect of the face (Figs. 1, Fig. 4). There was 1 nose and 1 mouth in the face, suggesting only partial duplication of facial structure. Neck appeared normal with no evidence of cystic hygroma. There was 1 trunk with normal chest and abdominal structures. The posterior arch was deficit in lower lumbar region with herniation of small amount of brain tissue and cerebrospinal fluid suggestive of small meningomyelocele (Figs. 2 and 6). Both the feet were medially deviated suggestive of bilateral club foot (Figs. 3 and 5). Only 1 pair of upper limbs and lower limbs was present. There was no evidence of duplication of thoracic or abdominal structure however, dextrocardia was noted during scan. Single anterior placenta was noted with normal amniotic fluid volume with maximum vertical pockets of 3.6cm. Fetal cardiac activity was regular with heart rate of 157bpm. So with constellation of findings of the duplicated frontal lobe with duplicated ventricular system, double thalami, partial absence of cranial vault, 4 eyes, small meningomyelocele, and bilateral club foot—the final diagnosis of diprosopus tetrophthalmus with acrania, spinal bifida and bilateral club foot was made. Couple counseling was provided regarding the poor prognosis of fetus due to various malformations, and termination of pregnancy was recommended. Post-abortus image of the fetus are shown in Fig. 4, Fig. 5, Fig. 6, which showed similar findings to the anomaly scan.

Discussion

Twin pregnancy can be monozygotic or dizygotic depending upon the number of initial zygotes formed during fertilization. Monozygotic twins can share placenta and amniotic cavity depending on time period of separation of the embryo resulting in dichorionic diamniotic, monochorionic diamniotic and monochorionic monoamniotic types of twin pregnancy [2]. Conjoint twins are rare types of twin that belongs to monozygotic monochorionic and monoamniotic category. Conjoint twins are defined according to parts of 2 twins fused with each other with thoracopagus (32.7%) being the most common and diprosopus (0.4%) being the least common variety [3]. Thoracopagus is defined as fusion in the thoracic region of twin whereas diprosopus is defined as presence of 2 faces in single head with variable degree of duplication of cranial and facial structure [1]. Prevalence of conjoint twin is 1 per 2800-200,000 deliveries with incidence of diprosopus being 1 in 180,000-15 million birth [3,1]. First presentation of diprosopus comes from an image with 2 heads found in the Neolithic sanctuary in Turkey, 6500 BC [4]. The exact etiology of Diprosopus is unknown, but there are various theories. One of which states that if there is defective oxidation between 15 and 25 days of implantation, there will be disruption in embryonic development, with defective splitting leading to diprosopus formation [4]. The recent theory states that it results from 2 different notochords which were initially destined to become separate twins but were too close to each other and failed to develop independently [4]. Approximately half of the cases of diprosopus are associated with neural tube defects as in our case which showed acrania and small meningomyelocele [5]. Acrania in our case may represent spectrum of acrania-exencephaly-anencephaly where acrania leads to exposure of brain tissue to amniotic fluid causing its destruction and degeneration resulting in anencephaly. In diprosopus, there are 2 faces-like structures on single head and trunk with malformations ranging from partial duplication of some structures like the nose, mandible, mouth to all facial structures [1]. Our case showed duplication of eyes, frontal lobe of brain, ventricular system of brain and thalami. Other anomalies commonly associated with this condition are duplication of the spine, cardiac defects, club foot, and duplication of abdominal structures like stomach [1]. In 1993 Rating categorized diprosopus in 3 groups as follow [6].Group I: Duplication takes place in upper half of face. There are 2 eyes and a nose to each face with common mouth.Group II: Duplication takes place in lower half of face. There is single face with 2 mouths.Group III: Duplication is symmetrical and may be parallel. There is duplication of nose and mouth but not of the eyes and ears.Our case most likely belongs to group (I) however, in our case there was only 1 nose which is possible in diprosopus as there are variable degrees of duplication of facial structure. Anomaly scan plays a vital role in the diagnosis of this complex anomaly and expert radiologists are needed to confirm the diagnosis thus guiding the management of the patient. MRI of the brain combined with MR angiography would allow for a more thorough evaluation of this case by better delineating the brain anatomy and vascular duplication if any, which are lacking in our case report. However our case highlights the importance of anomaly scans during pregnancy in low-income countries like Nepal, where people of rural areas rarely do anomaly scans during their pregnancy. Outcome of diprosopus is very poor and many neonates with diprosopus are stillborn. Treatment option in survivors includes surgical repair in selected incomplete variety and management of complications arising out of other anomalies [7].

Conclusion

Even though diprosopus is rarest type of conjoint twin, treating physician and radiologist should be aware of this condition for proper and timely management of this condition.

Patient consent

Written informed consent for publication of this case report was obtained from coples.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 20-year-old female, G1P0, at 18 weeks gestation.
- **Chief Complaint:** None, presented for a routine anomaly scan.
- **History of Present Illness:** None.
- **Past Medical History:** None, unremarkable. No drug intake apart from iron, calcium, and folic acid during early pregnancy.
- **Personal History:** Non-alcoholic and non-smoker.
- **Family History:** None reported.
- **Physical Examination:** Stable vitals; normal clinical examination.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Routine lab parameters (CBC, serology) normal.
  - Dating scan at 7 weeks revealed normal cardiac activity with no abnormalities.
  - **Anomaly Scan Findings:**
    - Duplicated frontal lobe with duplicated ventricles, dilated ventricular system, thinned brain parenchyma, and acrania.
    - 2 faces with 4 eyes (2 midline, 2 lateral), single nose and mouth, partial duplication of facial structures.
    - Single trunk with normal thoracic and abdominal organs.
    - Small meningomyelocele.
    - Bilateral club foot.
    - Dextrocardia noted, but no duplication of thoracic or abdominal structures.
    - Single anterior placenta with normal amniotic fluid volume.
    - Fetal heart rate: 157 bpm.
  - Diagnosis: Diprosopus tetrophthalmus (infant with 2 faces), acrania, spinal bifida, and bilateral clubfoot.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- **Step 1:** Assessment of the duplicated craniofacial and brain structures.
  - The findings of a partially duplicated frontal lobe, ventricles, and facial anomalies (4 eyes, single nose) along with acrania suggest a condition resulting from abnormal splitting of monozygotic monoamniotic twins, narrowing the possibilities to diprosopus.
  - Exclusion of other neural tube defects (e.g., isolated acrania, anencephaly) was possible due to the associated face and brain duplication.
- **Step 2:** Evaluation of other congenital anomalies.
  - Meningomyelocele and bilateral clubfoot were consistent with known malformations associated with diprosopus.
  - No thoracoabdominal duplications were seen, ruling out more generalized forms of conjoint twin anomalies (e.g., thoracopagus).
- **Step 3:** Specific classification of diprosopus.
  - Classifying based on the presence of two closely placed faces with shared facial and cranial components aligns with Group I diprosopus.
- Based on these findings, other possibilities such as teratomas, parasitic twins, or isolated neural tube defects were excluded in favor of the rare diagnosis of diprosopus tetrophthalmus.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Diprosopus tetrophthalmus with acrania, spinal bifida, and bilateral club foot. The diagnosis is supported by the characteristic craniofacial and neural anomalies on anomaly scan, coupled with their association with this rare form of monozygotic twinning.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Diprosopus tetrophthalmus with acrania, spinal bifida, and bilateral club foot.

results.content:
### Resoning:

**Step 1: Understanding the Anomaly Scan Findings**

First, I need to understand all the findings from the anomaly scan. The patient is a 20-year-old female, G1P0, at 18 weeks gestation, who presented for a routine anomaly scan. There are no chief complaints or significant medical history. The anomaly scan revealed several abnormalities:

- Duplicated frontal lobe with duplicated ventricles, dilated ventricular system, thinned brain parenchyma, and acrania.

- Two faces with four eyes (two midline and two lateral), a single nose and mouth, and partial duplication of facial structures.

- A single trunk with normal thoracic and abdominal organs.

- A small meningomyelocele.

- Bilateral clubfoot.

- Dextrocardia noted, but no duplication of thoracic or abdominal structures.

- Single anterior placenta with normal amniotic fluid volume.

- Fetal heart rate: 157 bpm.

**Step 2: Identifying the Key Features**

The key features here are the duplicated facial structures, specifically the presence of two faces with four eyes, which is referred to as diprosopus tetrophthalmus. Additionally, there are neural tube defects such as acrania and meningomyelocele, as well as other anomalies like bilateral clubfoot and dextrocardia.

**Step 3: Understanding Diprosopus**

Diprosopus is a rare congenital anomaly characterized by the duplication of facial structures. It is considered a form of holoprosencephaly, which is a failure of the forebrain to divide into two hemispheres during embryonic development. However, in this case, there is duplicated frontal lobe and ventricles, which seems contradictory to typical holoprosencephaly.

**Step 4: Considering Neural Tube Defects**

The presence of acrania and meningomyelocele indicates defects in neurulation, specifically problems in the closure of the neural tube. These defects can occur due to genetic factors, environmental factors, or a combination of both. Folic acid supplementation is known to reduce the risk of neural tube defects, but in this case, the mother was taking folic acid during early pregnancy.

**Step 5: Relating Brain and Facial Abnormalities**

The duplicated frontal lobe and ventricles suggest a complex defect in brain development. The thinned brain parenchyma and dilated ventricular system could indicate hydrocephalus or other cerebral anomalies. Acrania is a severe neural tube defect where there is absence of the cranial vault, which is incompatible with postnatal life.

**Step 6: Evaluating the Fetal Condition**

Given the severity of the anomalies, particularly the acrania and the complex facial duplications, the prognosis for this fetus is likely to be poor. Acrania is fatal, as it leads to exposure of the brain and its degeneration. The presence of other anomalies such as clubfoot and dextrocardia adds to the complexity.

**Step 7: Considering Differential Diagnoses**

I need to consider whether these findings could be attributed to other conditions. For instance, holoprosencephaly can present with facial duplications, but typically involves other brain anomalies. Given the duplicated frontal lobes and ventricles, it might represent a variant of holoprosencephaly or another type of brain malformation.

**Step 8: Reviewing the Diagnosis Provided**

The diagnosis provided is diprosopus tetrophthalmus (infant with two faces), acrania, spinal bifida (meningomyelocele), and bilateral clubfoot. This seems accurate based on the scan findings. However, I should confirm whether these conditions can occur together and what might be the underlying cause.

**Step 9: Considering Genetic Counseling**

Given the severity and rarity of these anomalies, genetic counseling would be advisable. There may be underlying genetic abnormalities or teratogenic exposures that contributed to these defects. Although the mother reports no drug intake apart from iron, calcium, and folic acid, and no alcohol or smoking, other factors could be at play.

**Step 10: Advising Next Steps**

In this scenario, the next steps would involve discussing the prognosis with the parents, considering the palliative nature of the condition due to acrania. Options for termination of pregnancy may be considered, depending on the legal and ethical frameworks in place. Additionally, further genetic testing or counseling could be offered to understand the risk for future pregnancies.

### Answer:

Diprosopus tetrophthalmus with acrania, meningomyelocele, bilateral clubfoot, and dextrocardia.

reasoning_eval.step: First, I need to understand all the findings from the anomaly scan. The patient is a 20-year-old female, G1P0, at 18 weeks gestation, who presented for a routine anomaly scan. There are no chief complaints or significant medical history. The anomaly scan revealed several abnormalities:
- Duplicated frontal lobe with duplicated ventricles, dilated ventricular system, thinned brain parenchyma, and acrania.
- Two faces with four eyes (two midline and two lateral), a single nose and mouth, and partial duplication of facial structures.
- A single trunk with normal thoracic and abdominal organs.
- Dextrocardia noted, but no duplication of thoracic or abdominal structures.
- Single anterior placenta with normal amniotic fluid volume.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Assessment of the duplicated craniofacial and brain structures shows a partially duplicated frontal lobe, ventricles, and facial anomalies (4 eyes, single nose) along with acrania, suggesting diprosopus and excluding other neural tube defects like isolated acrania or anencephaly.

Evaluation of other congenital anomalies finds meningomyelocele and bilateral clubfoot consistent with diprosopus, while no thoracoabdominal duplications rule out more generalized conjoint twin anomalies.
Specific classification of diprosopus based on two closely placed faces with shared facial and cranial components aligns with Group I diprosopus.
Other possibilities such as teratomas, parasitic twins, or isolated neural tube defects are excluded in favor of the rare diagnosis of diprosopus tetrophthalmus.
Final diagnosis: Diprosopus tetrophthalmus with acrania, spinal bifida, and bilateral club foot.
